content.assessment: Networked clustering and multi-service hubs propose a lateral institutional solution that does not require altering who holds closure authority, potentially making it more administratively compatible with the existing tankerületi structure than proposals requiring authority transfer. Co-location with kindergarten services is institutionally coherent given that kindergarten coverage gaps are governed by the same national enrollment-threshold framework as primary schools, suggesting a shared administrative logic. However, the proposal's reliance on the Erdőkürt case—the only documented Hungarian example—as evidence of feasibility signals that success under current institutions has so far depended on rare local social capital rather than replicable administrative mechanisms.
opportunities: The 2026 reform's restoration of some principal/municipal input could provide an institutional entry point for formalizing inter-municipal clustering arrangements
strengths: Does not require altering the tankerületi centers' maintenance authority, making it more administratively compatible with existing governance than proposals requiring authority redistribution

Kindergarten and primary coverage gaps are governed by the same enrollment-threshold national framework, so joint hub co-location has a coherent institutional logic rather than crossing incompatible regulatory regimes
threats: Enrollment-threshold-based closure criteria (8-child minimum for kindergartens, similar class-size minimums for primary) could still be applied by tankerületi centers to networked schools individually, unless clustering explicitly changes how thresholds are counted across the network
weaknesses: The only documented Hungarian precedent (Erdőkürt) succeeded through organic social capital rather than formal networked structure, so it is unclear whether formal clustering can substitute for this without altering the underlying authority and funding structure
